nativeRange.habitat: McCosker's Flasher Wrasse is found on outer reef slopes and rubble zones, typically staying near the bottom over mixed coral, algae, and rubble substrates. Females tend to gather in small groups, and males actively patrol among them, putting on intense flashing courtship displays.

decorPreferences: abundant live rock with refuge crevices
tight-fitting lid (the species is a known jumper)

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on zooplankton and small benthic invertebrates in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafood, and high-quality marine flake or pellet foods. Tends to do best with several small feedings per day, in keeping with its high-metabolism planktivore habit.

avoidWith: additional male flasher wrasses in the same display

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by jeyre (iNaturalist), 1024x726. Tack-sharp full-body male in nuptial display showing the red-orange body, electric-blue lateral stripes and flared red dorsal/anal fins — the strongest diagnostic shot.
Photo by Debra Baker (iNaturalist/GBIF), 1203x922. Sharp, clean single individual (female/subadult phase), whole fish in frame — adds a non-display colour form for variety.
Photo by Debra Baker (iNaturalist/GBIF), 2048x1536. High-res vivid male showing red-orange body with electric-blue facial/head striping; whole fish visible though slightly tucked into reef.

summary: A small, brilliantly coloured Indo-West Pacific flasher wrasse, with males displaying intense reds, oranges and electric-blue body stripes that flare during courtship. Generally hardy, peaceful, and considered reef-safe.
careNotes: Best housed in an established reef or peaceful FOWLR aquarium with abundant live rock for refuge and open mid-water swimming space. A tight-fitting lid or mesh screen is widely regarded as essential — like most flasher and fairy wrasses, this species has been reported to jump from open-top tanks. Generally considered reef-safe and peaceful toward unrelated fishes, but only one male is recommended per display; multiple males will typically fight. Often kept as a single male with one or several females (a harem), which encourages the male's full courtship flashing display in which he intensifies his colours and flares his elongated dorsal and anal fins. Like most labrids, the species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite — under social pressure a dominant female can transition to male. Tends to be a constant grazer in the wild; multiple small feedings per day are generally advised in captivity to maintain condition and colour.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Paracheilinus flasher wrasses is considered very difficult and not meaningfully achieved in home aquaria. In the wild the species is oviparous with distinct pair-spawning during the male's flashing courtship display.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care level, temperament, reef compatibility, water parameters (temp 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), max size 3.25 in (~8 cm), 55 US gal minimum tank, jumper warning, harem/one-male-per-display behaviour, and courtship flashing description.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), Indo-West Pacific distribution (western Indian Ocean to Fiji), depth range 5–40 m (recorded to 50 m), maximum length 8.0 cm TL, oviparous reproduction with distinct pairing, and preferred temperature 25–28°C. Trophic level 3.4 confirms planktivore/carnivore diet.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning has not been meaningfully achieved in home aquaria. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females; under social pressure, a dominant female may transition and take on the male role.
